snippets.pull_quote: I've got my clients, I can do plenty of pipe, then I'm full. Three to six months later I come out and it's like, shit, where's my pipeline? I haven't posted, haven't done any outreach.
snippets.website_angle: Name the #1 fractional pain point — pipeline dries up the moment you're heads-down with clients.

snippets.pull_quote: I'm still not running any outbound campaigns, which is fucking insane. I have this conversation on a weekly basis.
snippets.website_angle: Even experienced operators know what they should do — and still don't do it. Speak to the knowing-doing gap.

snippets.pull_quote: The single shingle operator — that rings true. You're on your own, and when you're booked out, everything else stops.
snippets.website_angle: Fractionals have no team to delegate pipeline work to. Position the product as the missing support layer.

snippets.pull_quote: Context switching is a real challenge. I've got three fractional clients and a couple of coaching clients — I wouldn't have been able to do that without something running that context for me.
snippets.website_angle: Highlight how juggling multiple clients fragments attention — and how the right system holds it together.

snippets.pull_quote: If the client's too busy, you get drift — and then they get very unhappy. That's one I've learned the hard way.
snippets.website_angle: Surface the silent risk: client relationships erode when check-ins slip. Position consistent visibility as protection.

snippets.pull_quote: When the shit hits the fan working nights, my working-on-the-business stuff gets pushed. Client goals get better down if it gets busy.
snippets.website_angle: Business development is always the first casualty of a busy week. Show how to protect it automatically.

snippets.pull_quote: The reason Claude works for me is it removes the friction of putting shit in there. I just hit double tap and talk at it. Calendar tools? I end up with calendar overwhelm and switch it off.
snippets.website_angle: Fractionals abandon tools that demand manual input. Lead with zero-friction capture as a core product promise.

snippets.pull_quote: The best time to sell to fractionals is just before or just after they leave corporate — they're still cashed up and think dropping $500 on a tool is nothing. That window closes fast.
snippets.website_angle: Useful for GTM targeting copy — speak to the transition moment before the feast-or-famine reality sets in.

snippets.pull_quote: Having that playbook means I'm not feeling overwhelmed or anxious midweek because I don't know what I'm doing. I got that in place — urgent work blows up and it's okay.
snippets.website_angle: Frame structured workflows not just as productivity tools but as emotional stabilisers for solo operators.

snippets.pull_quote: It's not a high volume game. I only need 10 to 12 good clients a year. But that means every missed opportunity actually matters.
snippets.website_angle: Reframe pipeline urgency — small numbers mean zero margin for error. Each lost lead is significant.

snippets.pull_quote: The rule of thumb everybody should be doing: LinkedIn content, because we're all positioning ourselves as experts. If that's not filling your pipe, you need to be doing something else.
snippets.website_angle: Validate LinkedIn as table stakes, then show what fills the gap when organic alone isn't enough.

snippets.pull_quote: Experienced campaigners armed with context-heavy AI tools, absolutely smashing productivity — that sounds a lot like fractionals. That's the future.
snippets.website_angle: Position the product as the AI layer built specifically for seasoned operators, not generalist productivity apps.
